_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows David Eck's Introduction to Programming Using Java. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the source guides curriculum order only.
title: Exceptions
summary: Handle expected failures without hiding real bugs.
refresher: An exception is Java's way to report that something went wrong while the program was running. Use `try` around the risky work and `catch` for the failure you know how to handle. Do not catch everything just to make the error disappear.

blocks.body: An exception means the program hit a problem while running. Maybe a file was missing. Maybe input was not a number. Java can stop and hand that problem to code that knows how to respond.

blocks.caption: The catch type matches the conversion failure. Code after the failed parse inside try is skipped, then execution continues after catch.
blocks.output: Credits must be a whole number
blocks.body: `try` holds the risky work. `catch` handles a specific kind of failure. The goal is not to hide the problem. The goal is to recover when recovery makes sense.

Runtime exceptions can compile without a catch. Checked exceptions, including IOException, must be caught or listed in a method's `throws` clause. That rule forces file and network failures into the method design.
blocks.caption: The catch block handles bad number input and gives the user a clear message.
Catching Exception can mislabel a NullPointerException as bad input. The narrow catch handles only the case the message describes.
blocks.body: Do not wrap huge sections of code in one catch-all block. If everything is inside the try, it becomes harder to tell which line actually failed.

blocks.title: Keep the cause when adding context
blocks.body: If you create a new exception, pass the original one as its cause. Without that link, the stack trace loses the failure that started the chain and makes diagnosis harder.

blocks.why: The text does not match Java's integer format. The method reports that content problem with NumberFormatException.

blocks.body: A finally block runs after try and catch on either path. Try-with-resources is usually clearer for objects that implement AutoCloseable because closing becomes part of the declaration.

blocks.prompt: What must a method do with a checked IOException?
blocks.code: String load(Path path) { /* reads a file */ }
choices: Catch it or declare it with `throws`
Ignore it because all exceptions are optional
Place it in a static field
blocks.why: Java's compiler tracks checked exceptions. The method must handle the IOException or state in its signature that a caller is responsible for it.

blocks.title: Exception checklist
items: Use try for work that may fail.
Catch the specific failure you can handle.
Do not silence errors you still need to fix.
Preserve useful error details while debugging.
